terms.definition: The sentient self: a constitutionally complete, immortal unit that works through the body — not brain activity alone.
Omnipresence — the formless, all-pervasive, non-transforming ground in which all units are saturated.
In Madhyasth Darshan: duration of unit-activity (*kriya*); not an independent cosmic container.
Formation or transmission of understanding across generations — education-*sanskar* carries evidenced understanding at the knowledge order.
Reality as the pervasive ground (*satta*) and all units (*ikai*) inseparably present together — beginningless, without creation from nothing.
A countable, bounded entity in nature — insentient (*jada*) or sentient (*chaitanya*).
Existence as coexistence of formless *satta* and active units — beginningless and endless.
Unit-activity; the basis of change and, in sentient nature, of duration (*kaal*).
The ever-present bond in which each unit is soaked in Omnipresence; inherent energy and regulation belong in the unit.
Constitutional completeness — when a developed atom integrates required particles and crosses irreversibly into sentient *jeevan*.
Knowledge in several distinct senses in the texts — ontological given, intelligibility-ground, telos of fulfilled relationships, and unfolding through awakened *jeevan*.
Unfolding of knowledge in thought and conduct — an activity exclusive to awakened *jeevan*, not Omnipresence or insentient matter.
Realisation Knowledge — the ontological given in saturated units: form, properties, essential nature, *dharma*, and orderliness.
Authentic conduct as living proof that understanding has taken hold — the outward test of complete knowing.
Co-existentialism — the philosophy founded by Shri A. Nagraj, holding reality as coexistence of Omnipresence and real units.
The four orders of nature — material, bio/pranic, animal, and knowledge/human — real developmental plateaus.
Relationship — definite mutuality with expectations predetermined toward completeness.
Effort–motion–result — the single inseparable triad in which all change appears.
Perspective or evaluative lens — in *jeevan*, six built-in perspectives from lower *priya*/*hita*/*labh* to humane *nyaya*/*dharma*/*satya*.
In Madhyasth Darshan: deliberation and intention toward resolution in thought — one of three humane evaluative domains.
Justice — complete relational activity: recognise, fulfil, evaluate, mutual satisfaction.
Realisation perspective — whether assessment is grounded in coexistence as it is.
Resolution — understanding and relational closure without residue; joint evidence of complete happiness.
Delusion — living by inherited belief rather than understanding, rooted in identifying *jeevan* with the body.
Mysteriousness — removable deficit in the knower's reflection, not a permanent opacity of nature.
Advaita: dependent appearance — neither absolutely real nor sheer nothing; valid at *vyavahara*, sublated at *paramartha*.
Advaita: shared empirical order — bodies, law, ethics, scripture; robust but sublated at highest realization.
Advaita: absolute level of truth; Brahman alone is absolutely real.
Advaita: the innermost Self; in Madhyasth Darshan the sentient nucleus within constitutionally complete *jeevan*.
Advaita: the sole absolute reality — being, consciousness, bliss (*sat-chit-ananda*), one without a second.
A faculty of *jeevan* — imagination and selection; part of the orbital structure with *buddhi*, *vritti*, and *chitta*.
Discriminative faculty in *jeevan* — part of the sentient unit's orbital structure with *mun*, *vritti*, and *chitta*.
Store or residue faculty in *jeevan* — part of the orbital structure; misalignment among faculties yields intellectual mystery.
Modification or tendency in *jeevan* — one of the faculties whose alignment supports or obstructs understanding.
Nature — the realm of real units in coexistence; law-governed and beginningless in Madhyasth Darshan.
The knowledge/human order — the level of nature able to know and evaluate every other level.
